meta.notes: AI-generated practice content aligned to the (ISC)² CISSP Exam Outline effective April 15, 2024. Requires human SME review.

questions.stem: Which OSI layer does ARP poisoning operate at?

options.B: Layer 2 (Data Link)

questions.rationale: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) operates at OSI Layer 2, the data link layer, because it maps IP addresses (Layer 3) to hardware MAC addresses (Layer 2) for local network delivery. ARP poisoning attacks inject false ARP mappings into a target's cache, causing traffic destined for one host to be redirected to the attacker. The attack lives at L2 because ARP itself is an L2 protocol that does not cross routers — it operates only within a broadcast domain. Option A (physical) is too low; physical-layer attacks involve cable tapping or electromagnetic emanations. Option C (network) is one layer too high; IP spoofing operates at L3 because it manipulates the IP header, but ARP poisoning does not touch the IP header — it manipulates the MAC-to-IP mapping at L2. Option D (transport) concerns TCP/UDP attacks. The CISSP exam tests attack-to-layer mapping because it drives control selection: defenses against L2 attacks must be at L2 (Dynamic ARP Inspection, 802.1X, port security), not at L3 firewalls. Memorize the mapping: ARP → L2, IP spoof → L3, SYN flood → L4.

questions.stem: TLS 1.3 mandates which of the following properties for cipher suites?

options.B: Forward secrecy via ephemeral key exchange (ECDHE or DHE)

questions.rationale: TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446, 2018) made forward secrecy mandatory by removing static RSA key exchange and only supporting ephemeral key exchange methods: ECDHE (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral) and DHE (Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral). Forward secrecy means that compromise of a long-term server private key does not allow decryption of past recorded TLS sessions, because the session keys were derived from ephemeral values that were never stored. This is a major security improvement over TLS 1.2, which allowed static RSA key exchange and did not guarantee forward secrecy across all cipher suites. TLS 1.3 also removed CBC-mode ciphers, SHA-1, RC4, compression, and renegotiation. Option A is exactly what TLS 1.3 removed. Option C is wrong — CBC was removed in favor of AEAD modes (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, AES-CCM). Option D is wrong — MD5 has been removed from TLS for years. The CISSP exam tests TLS 1.3 specifically because it is the current standard and because its security properties differ significantly from TLS 1.2. Related: TLS 1.3 also reduced the handshake from 2-RTT to 1-RTT, and added optional 0-RTT resumption (with caveats about replay protection).

questions.stem: Which IPsec mode is typically used for site-to-site VPNs between two gateways, encapsulating the entire original IP packet?

options.B: Tunnel mode

questions.rationale: IPsec has two modes: Transport and Tunnel. Tunnel mode encapsulates the entire original IP packet (including its header) inside a new IP packet with new headers, then protects the whole thing with ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload). This is the standard mode for site-to-site VPNs because it hides the internal addressing from the public network and allows two sites to appear adjacent even though traffic crosses the internet between them. Transport mode, by contrast, keeps the original IP header and inserts the ESP header between the IP header and the payload, protecting only the payload. Transport mode is used for host-to-host encryption where the original source and destination are both endpoints and routing does not need to be hidden. Option A (Transport) is wrong for the scenario. Option C (AH-only) is not a standard mode description — AH is a protocol variant that provides authentication without encryption, and can be used with either mode; but IPsec AH-only is uncommon because it does not provide confidentiality. Option D (Null encryption) is a testing/debugging option and is not a mode. The CISSP exam tests IPsec modes because the distinction is central to VPN design. Memorize: Tunnel for site-to-site (between gateways), Transport for host-to-host.

questions.stem: Which wireless security standard uses SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) to replace the pre-shared key handshake?

options.B: WPA

questions.rationale: WPA3 introduced SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals), sometimes called Dragonfly, to replace WPA2's four-way handshake with PSK. SAE is resistant to offline dictionary attacks: where WPA2 allowed an attacker to capture the handshake and then run an offline brute-force attack against the pre-shared key, SAE requires the attacker to run one online attempt per guess, each of which is visible to the network operator. SAE also provides forward secrecy, meaning that even if the password is later compromised, past session keys cannot be recovered. WPA3 also introduced Opportunistic Wireless Encryption (OWE) for open networks (encryption without authentication), and a 192-bit mode for high-assurance enterprise deployments. Option A (WEP) has been broken for decades and never used SAE. Option B (WPA) used TKIP and was replaced quickly by WPA2. Option C (WPA2) used the four-way handshake with PSK and is the standard SAE replaces. The CISSP exam tests WPA3 features because it is the current wireless standard and because KRACK (2017) demonstrated that WPA2's four-way handshake could be attacked via key reinstallation. Memorize: WPA3 = SAE = forward secrecy + offline-dictionary-resistance.

questions.stem: Which protocol is PRIMARILY used in 802.1X port-based network authentication?

options.B: DHCP

questions.rationale: 802.1X is a port-based network access control standard that authenticates devices before granting them network access. It defines three roles: the supplicant (the client device), the authenticator (the switch or access point), and the authentication server. The authentication server is almost always a RADIUS (Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service) server, which receives EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol) messages encapsulated in RADIUS and validates credentials. EAP methods include EAP-TLS (certificate-based, strongest), PEAP (tunneled, used for password-based MSCHAPv2), EAP-TTLS (similar to PEAP), and others. Option A captures RADIUS as the primary protocol. Option B (DHCP) is unrelated — DHCP issues IP addresses after network access is granted. Option C (ARP) is unrelated — ARP is an L2 resolution protocol. Option D (SNMP) is a network management protocol unrelated to authentication. The CISSP exam tests 802.1X because it is the authentication backbone of enterprise wired and wireless networks and because it is the technical mechanism underlying NAC (Network Access Control). Related: TACACS+ is an older Cisco protocol used for administrative authentication, DIAMETER is a RADIUS successor for AAA, and 802.1X integrates with modern identity providers via RADIUS proxying.

questions.stem: A network architect is designing a new office network. Sensitive financial systems must be strictly isolated from general-purpose user workstations. Which segmentation approach BEST achieves the isolation?

options.A: Place all systems on the same subnet and rely on host-based firewalls
options.B: Create separate VLANs for financial systems and user workstations, enforce inter-VLAN traffic through a firewall with least-privilege rules, and add monitoring on inter-VLAN flows
options.C: Rely on physical security of the server room

questions.rationale: Network segmentation is the foundational control for limiting blast radius in a breach. The correct approach combines logical separation (VLANs or subnets), enforced routing through a firewall between zones with least-privilege rules, and monitoring of the flows between zones. This creates a policy enforcement point where every inter-zone packet is inspected, logged, and subject to policy — which is what makes the zones actually isolated as opposed to merely labeled. Option B captures this. Option A (same subnet with host firewalls) fails on two counts: host firewalls can be disabled, and L2 attacks like ARP poisoning can operate within a single subnet regardless of host firewall configuration. Option C (physical security) is a legitimate control but does nothing about network-based attacks from compromised workstations. Option D (stronger passwords) is unrelated to segmentation. The CISSP exam tests segmentation because it is the single highest-leverage network security control and because many real-world breaches (Target 2013, Equifax 2017) were exacerbated by flat networks. A more mature design adds micro-segmentation at the workload level for high-sensitivity systems, eliminating implicit trust between systems on the same VLAN.

questions.stem: An organization's remote workers need secure access to internal applications. The organization is evaluating traditional IPsec VPN, SSL VPN, and Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA). Which approach BEST aligns with a zero trust architecture?

options.A: Traditional IPsec VPN giving full network access
options.B: SSL VPN giving full network access
options.C: ZTNA providing per-application access based on identity, device posture, and policy evaluation per request
options.D: Open the internal applications to the public internet

questions.rationale: ZTNA (Zero Trust Network Access) provides per-application tunneling where each request is authenticated and authorized against current identity, device posture, and contextual policy. Users never 'join' the network; they are granted access to specific applications for specific sessions. This aligns with zero trust principles by eliminating implicit network-level trust and enforcing least privilege at the application level. In contrast, both traditional IPsec VPN and SSL VPN typically grant full network access once authenticated, meaning a compromised user or device can move laterally across all internal resources. Option C captures the zero-trust-aligned answer. Option A (IPsec VPN with full access) is the opposite of zero trust. Option B (SSL VPN with full access) is the same problem with a different protocol. Option D (public exposure) is reckless. The CISSP exam increasingly tests ZTNA because it is the practical expression of zero trust for remote access, it addresses lateral movement (the root cause of many ransomware incidents), and it maps to NIST SP 800-207 zero trust architecture. Implementations include identity-aware proxies, SASE (Secure Access Service Edge) platforms, and standalone ZTNA products. The key property: per-application access, not per-network.

questions.stem: A security team detects repeated TCP SYN packets to a production server with no corresponding ACKs. The connection table fills up and legitimate users cannot connect. Which defense is MOST DIRECTLY designed to address this attack?

options.B: SYN cookies to avoid allocating state until a valid ACK arrives

questions.rationale: The scenario describes a SYN flood: an attacker sends TCP SYN packets with spoofed source addresses, causing the server to allocate half-open connection state while waiting for ACKs that never arrive. When the connection table fills, legitimate connections are dropped — a denial of service. SYN cookies are the canonical defense: the server responds to SYN with a SYN-ACK but does not allocate connection state; instead, it encodes the connection state in the initial sequence number using a cryptographic hash. When a real ACK arrives, the server validates the cookie and then allocates state. An attacker cannot forge a valid cookie without receiving the SYN-ACK, so spoofed attackers cannot complete the handshake. Option B captures this. Option A (FDE) addresses data at rest on endpoints and has no bearing on network-level attacks. Option C (passwords) is unrelated. Option D (DNSSEC) addresses DNS integrity, not TCP state exhaustion. The CISSP exam tests SYN flood and SYN cookies because they are a textbook example of a protocol vulnerability solved by an elegant cryptographic defense. Related: modern servers also use rate limiting, connection tracking limits, and upstream scrubbing services for volumetric SYN floods that overwhelm even cookie-based defenses.

questions.stem: A company's DNS records are being modified by unauthorized parties, and users are being redirected to fraudulent websites. Which of the following BEST addresses the root cause?

options.A: Force users to use Google Public DNS
options.B: Implement DNSSEC to sign authoritative DNS records and configure recursive resolvers to validate signatures, plus secure the registrar account with strong authentication and registrar lock

questions.rationale: The scenario could involve two attack paths: (1) authoritative DNS records are being modified (via compromised DNS server, registrar hijack, or compromised DNS admin credentials), and/or (2) recursive resolvers are being poisoned. DNSSEC addresses both when implemented end-to-end: signing authoritative records makes unauthorized modification detectable, and validating signatures at the resolver prevents poisoned caches from being accepted. In addition to DNSSEC, the registrar account must be secured with strong authentication (MFA is essential), and registrar lock (preventing transfers without additional authentication) adds protection against registrar-level hijacks. Option B captures this layered approach. Option A (public DNS) is unrelated to authoritative DNS modifications and does not address the root cause. Option C (DoH) addresses resolver privacy and encryption but does not address integrity; DoH protects the query-response channel from network-level tampering but not from authoritative compromise. Option D (disable DNS) is not operationally viable. The CISSP exam tests DNSSEC because it is the primary defense against DNS integrity attacks, and because several major DNS hijack campaigns in 2019 (e.g., targeting Middle Eastern governments and organizations) demonstrated the real-world impact of weak DNS security. DoH/DoT provide confidentiality and channel encryption; DNSSEC provides integrity and authentication of the records themselves. The two are complementary, not alternatives.

questions.stem: A security engineer is selecting a firewall for a new internet-facing web application. The application processes HTTP traffic and must be protected from SQL injection, XSS, and SSRF attacks. Which firewall type is MOST APPROPRIATE?

options.B: Web Application Firewall (WAF) with modern rule sets like OWASP Core Rule Set
options.D: Host-based firewall on the application server

questions.rationale: The attacks listed (SQL injection, XSS, SSRF) are application-layer attacks against HTTP. A WAF is specifically designed to inspect HTTP requests and responses and block attacks at the application layer. Modern WAFs use signature-based rules (OWASP Core Rule Set is the most widely deployed open ruleset), behavioral analysis, and often ML-based anomaly detection. They can understand HTTP semantics, parse query parameters and JSON bodies, and apply context-aware policies. Option B captures the correct choice. Option A (stateless packet filter) operates at L3/L4 and has no visibility into HTTP payload; it cannot distinguish a SQL injection from a normal query. Option C (network IDS) may detect some of these attacks but does not block inline (IPS would), and in any case is less specialized for HTTP than a WAF. Option D (host firewall) restricts network access but does not inspect HTTP content. The CISSP exam tests WAF selection because it is a common choice for internet-facing applications and because it illustrates the principle of applying the right control at the right layer. Related: WAFs are not a substitute for secure coding. The correct defense-in-depth pattern combines a WAF at the network edge with secure coding practices (input validation, parameterized queries, output encoding, CSP headers) at the application level.

questions.stem: An organization's security policy requires that all administrative SSH access to servers be logged and that credentials not traverse the network. Which configuration BEST satisfies the requirement?

options.A: SSH with password authentication over VPN
options.B: SSH with public-key authentication (preferably certificates) and session recording via a bastion host
options.D: Direct SSH from any workstation with a shared account

questions.rationale: Public-key SSH authentication does not transmit the user's credential over the network — the private key never leaves the client, and the authentication exchange proves possession of the key without revealing it. SSH certificates (a signed credential issued by a trusted CA, with explicit validity, principals, and options) improve on raw key pairs by supporting centralized issuance and revocation. A bastion host centralizes SSH access so that administrative sessions are logged and recorded at a single chokepoint, satisfying the logging requirement. Option B captures both requirements. Option A (password over VPN) still transmits the password (though over an encrypted channel) and does not provide the key-based non-transmission the policy requires. Option C (Telnet with encrypted passwords) is a contradiction — Telnet is plaintext and should not be used. Option D (shared account) defeats accountability and logging. The CISSP exam tests administrative access patterns because they are high-value targets and because the correct design combines multiple principles: key-based auth (no credential transmission), bastion hosts (centralized logging and session recording), minimum privilege (no shared accounts, per-user access), and often MFA on the bastion.

questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing network monitoring and notices that unusual outbound DNS queries are being generated from a server that does not normally query DNS. The queries are to unusual domains with high-entropy subdomains. Which attack is MOST LIKELY in progress?

options.B: DNS tunneling for data exfiltration or command-and-control

questions.rationale: The pattern described — unusual outbound DNS queries to unusual domains with high-entropy subdomains from a server that does not normally query DNS — is a classic signature of DNS tunneling. Attackers use DNS as a covert channel because DNS is almost always allowed outbound by firewalls. Data is encoded into subdomain labels (which can carry several tens of bytes per label) and the authoritative DNS server for the attacker's domain receives the queries, effectively receiving exfiltrated data or sending commands to the infected host. Option B captures this correctly. Option A (normal caching) does not produce unusual high-entropy subdomains. Option C (DHCP starvation) is an L2 attack unrelated to DNS. Option D (ICMP flood) is volumetric and uses ICMP, not DNS. The CISSP exam tests DNS tunneling because it is a common exfiltration technique and because DNS is one of the most under-monitored protocols in most networks. Defenses include: DNS query logging and analysis, entropy analysis of subdomain labels, rate limiting DNS queries per host, egress filtering that restricts DNS to approved resolvers only, DNS firewall/RPZ that blocks known malicious domains, and SIEM correlation of unusual DNS patterns with other indicators.

questions.stem: A company discovers that its wireless network's WPA2-PSK passphrase has been cracked via offline dictionary attack. The attacker captured a handshake from a legitimate user. Which network upgrade BEST addresses this vulnerability class?

options.B: Migrate to WPA3-SAE, which is resistant to offline dictionary attacks by design

questions.rationale: WPA2's four-way handshake can be captured by any nearby attacker and then used for offline dictionary attacks against the pre-shared key. Even strong passphrases are eventually crackable given enough compute. WPA3's SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) handshake is designed specifically to be resistant to offline dictionary attacks: each authentication attempt requires an online interaction with the network, and each attempt is rate-limited and observable to the network operator. WPA3 also provides forward secrecy, meaning that capturing a handshake does not enable decryption of past or future sessions even if the passphrase is later compromised. Option B captures the correct upgrade. Option A (rotate passphrase) is a temporary mitigation that slows the attacker but does not address the offline-attack weakness. Option C (disable SSID broadcast) is security theater — SSID is easily discovered from client probe requests and packet analysis. Option D (MAC filtering) is bypassable by sniffing and spoofing any authorized MAC. The CISSP exam tests WPA3 because it is the current standard and because KRACK (2017) demonstrated that WPA2 has additional weaknesses beyond offline dictionary attacks.

questions.stem: A CISO is reviewing a design for a new cloud-hosted multi-tier application. The tiers are web frontend, application servers, and database. Which network architecture BEST enforces separation of tiers?

options.A: All tiers in a single flat subnet
options.B: Separate subnets (VPC or equivalent) for each tier, with security groups permitting only the minimum required inter-tier traffic, default-deny egress, and database tier not directly reachable from the internet
options.C: All tiers in public subnets for simplicity
options.D: Database on the same subnet as the web tier with a host firewall

questions.rationale: A multi-tier application's security depends on strict separation of tiers with controlled inter-tier traffic. The correct architecture places each tier in its own subnet (or equivalent isolation in cloud providers), uses security groups or NACLs to permit only the minimum required inter-tier flows, defaults egress to deny, and ensures the database tier is not directly reachable from the internet — only from the application tier. The web tier may face the internet (through a load balancer or WAF), the application tier faces only the web tier and accesses only the database tier, and the database tier is isolated from the internet entirely. Option B captures this. Option A (flat subnet) eliminates the separation that segmentation provides. Option C (all public subnets) exposes the database to the internet directly, a severe design failure. Option D (same subnet with host firewall) is better than A but still leaves L2 attacks possible and depends entirely on host firewall integrity. The CISSP exam tests multi-tier segmentation because it is a fundamental application architecture pattern and because many real-world breaches involved database tiers that should have been isolated but were not. Related cloud concepts: VPC peering, PrivateLink/Private Service Connect, public vs private subnets, and network policies in Kubernetes.

questions.stem: An attacker is using ARP poisoning on a corporate LAN to intercept traffic. Which of the following is the MOST EFFECTIVE defense?

options.B: Enable Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on switches with DHCP snooping, combined with port security and 802.1X where applicable

questions.rationale: ARP poisoning is an L2 attack, and the correct defense is at L2. Dynamic ARP Inspection validates each ARP packet against a DHCP snooping database that records legitimate IP-to-MAC mappings as DHCP assignments are made. ARP packets inconsistent with the database are dropped. Port security limits the number of MAC addresses per switch port, defeating MAC flooding attacks. 802.1X authenticates devices before granting network access, so an unauthorized attacker cannot reach L2 at all. The combination provides layered defense at L2. Option B captures this correctly. Option A (at-rest encryption) addresses a different threat entirely. Option C (antivirus) may catch malware that enables the attack but does not prevent the L2 poisoning itself. Option D (password rotation) addresses credential theft, not L2 manipulation. The CISSP exam tests L2 defenses because candidates often default to firewalls (L3/L4) for network security and underweight the attacks that firewalls cannot see. The general principle: control placement must match attack layer. L2 attacks require L2 controls; L3 attacks require L3 controls; L7 attacks require L7 controls.

questions.stem: A security team wants to prevent malicious lateral movement after an initial compromise. Which design pattern MOST DIRECTLY addresses this goal?

options.B: Implement micro-segmentation with per-workload identity-aware policies, such that each workload is permitted only the specific inbound and outbound flows it legitimately requires

questions.rationale: Lateral movement — an attacker moving from an initial compromised host to other internal resources — is enabled by flat networks and over-permissive east-west traffic. Micro-segmentation addresses this by enforcing per-workload policies at the workload boundary: each server or container has a policy specifying exactly which other workloads it can talk to and what it can receive, with default-deny for everything else. A compromised workload cannot expand beyond its authorized reach because the other workloads reject unauthorized connections. Implementation approaches include host-based micro-segmentation agents, service mesh policies (Istio, Linkerd), cloud-native security groups and network policies (AWS SGs, Azure NSGs, GCP firewall rules, Kubernetes Network Policies), and dedicated micro-segmentation products (Illumio, Guardicore/Akamai, VMware NSX). Option B captures this. Option A (more firewalls) focuses on perimeter; lateral movement happens inside. Option C (antivirus) may prevent some compromise but does not stop lateral movement after compromise. Option D (passwords) is unrelated. The CISSP exam tests micro-segmentation because it is increasingly the answer to ransomware propagation and because NIST SP 800-207 zero trust architecture identifies it as a foundational control.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating whether to deploy an IDS or an IPS at the network perimeter. The operations team is concerned about false positives blocking legitimate traffic. Which approach is MOST APPROPRIATE for an initial deployment?

options.A: IPS in blocking mode immediately with aggressive rules
options.B: IDS (out-of-band monitoring) first to establish baseline and tune rules, then migrate to IPS in blocking mode after false-positive rate is acceptable

questions.rationale: The difference between IDS and IPS is that IDS detects and alerts while IPS detects and blocks inline. IPS is more effective when rules are well-tuned but can disrupt legitimate traffic if false positives are high. The mature deployment pattern starts with IDS mode (or IPS in monitor-only / alert-only mode) to establish a baseline, tune rules against real traffic, and verify that alerts correlate with actual attacks rather than benign activity. Once the false-positive rate is acceptable, the deployment can migrate to inline blocking mode (or IPS in blocking mode) with confidence that legitimate traffic will not be disrupted. Option B captures this graduated approach. Option A (IPS immediately aggressive) is a common cause of outages and operational friction, and often leads to the team disabling the IPS entirely. Option C (firewall alone) does not provide the application-aware detection that IDS/IPS offer. Option D (IDS permanently) sacrifices the blocking benefit when false positives have been tamed. The CISSP exam tests IDS/IPS deployment because it is a textbook example of balancing detection with operational impact, and because the deployment strategy matters as much as the technology choice.

questions.stem: A CISSP is asked to recommend a secure remote access solution for a fully remote workforce. The organization wants to avoid the operational complexity of full-tunnel VPNs and wants to enforce device posture checks. Which is the BEST recommendation?

options.A: Publish all applications directly to the internet
options.B: A ZTNA / SSE solution that enforces per-application access based on identity, device posture, and contextual policy
options.C: Physical tokens for every remote user

questions.rationale: For a remote-first workforce, ZTNA (Zero Trust Network Access) combined with SSE (Security Service Edge) is the modern best-practice. ZTNA provides per-application access rather than full-network access, which eliminates the lateral-movement risk of traditional VPNs. Device posture checking can be integrated via agent-based or agentless methods, ensuring that only compliant devices get access. Identity-aware proxies or identity-aware gateways sit between users and internal applications and evaluate policy on each request. SSE platforms bundle ZTNA with CASB, SWG, and DLP, providing a unified edge. Option B captures the modern answer. Option A (public internet exposure) is reckless and abandons access control. Option C (physical tokens) addresses one authentication factor but does not provide the architectural properties of ZTNA. Option D (split-tunnel IPsec) is a legacy approach that still grants full network access to the 'internal' side. The CISSP exam increasingly tests ZTNA because it is the practical zero trust remote access answer and because the remote-work shift has made it the fastest-growing category of enterprise security investment.

questions.stem: A CISSP reviews a network design and finds that the organization's BGP announcements can be hijacked because BGP is unauthenticated. Which technology is SPECIFICALLY designed to address BGP origin hijacking?

options.B: Resource Public Key Infrastructure (RPKI) with Route Origin Validation

questions.rationale: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the internet's interdomain routing protocol and is historically unauthenticated. BGP hijacks — where one AS announces prefixes belonging to another AS, redirecting traffic — have been a recurring problem (Pakistan/YouTube 2008, various Chinese and Russian incidents, accidental leaks by major ISPs). RPKI (Resource Public Key Infrastructure) provides cryptographic validation of BGP origin announcements: the legitimate holder of an IP prefix publishes a Route Origin Authorization (ROA) signed by the regional internet registry, and BGP routers can validate received announcements against the ROA database. Route Origin Validation (ROV) configured on routers drops or deprioritizes announcements that do not match. RPKI with ROV addresses origin hijacking; BGPSEC extends this to path validation but has seen limited deployment. Option B captures RPKI. Option A (DNSSEC) addresses DNS, not BGP. Option C (IPsec) is for payload encryption and does not validate routing announcements. Option D (TLS) is transport-layer, not routing-layer. The CISSP exam tests RPKI because it is the current answer to BGP hijacking and because BGP security remains one of the most under-addressed systemic risks on the internet.

questions.stem: A CISSP is asked to recommend a secure file-transfer mechanism for moving sensitive data between an organization and its external partners. Which approach is BEST?

options.B: SFTP or FTPS using mutual authentication and a dedicated account with logged, auditable transfers

questions.rationale: Secure file transfer between organizations requires confidentiality, integrity, authentication of both parties, and auditability. SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol) and FTPS (FTP over TLS) both provide transport encryption; the choice between them is usually operational preference (SFTP uses a single TCP connection over SSH; FTPS uses TLS and may require multiple ports). Mutual authentication (SSH keys or client certificates for the external party, rather than shared passwords) provides strong authentication. A dedicated account per external partner with least-privilege access to a specific directory, combined with logging of every transfer operation, provides auditability. Option B captures these elements. Option A (plain FTP) transmits credentials and data in the clear. Option C (email) lacks integrity controls and is typically not suitable for regulated data. Option D (unauthenticated HTTP) has no authentication at all. The CISSP exam tests secure file transfer because it is a common need and because insecure transfer mechanisms remain common in B2B contexts. Related: managed file transfer (MFT) products add workflow, approval, encryption at rest on the transfer server, and integration with DLP — appropriate for high-volume regulated use cases.

questions.stem: A penetration tester demonstrates that an attacker can perform VLAN hopping via double tagging to reach a VLAN that should be isolated. Which switch configuration change MOST DIRECTLY addresses this?

options.B: Ensure that the native VLAN on trunk ports is not used by any access ports, or explicitly disable native-VLAN traffic; also disable DTP (dynamic trunking protocol) and force access ports to non-trunking mode

questions.rationale: VLAN hopping via double tagging exploits the fact that some switches strip only the outer 802.1Q tag on ingress, leaving the inner tag intact. If the attacker sends a frame with an outer tag matching the native VLAN and an inner tag matching the target VLAN, and if the trunk port preserves the inner tag, the frame reaches the target VLAN. The defense is to ensure the native VLAN on trunk ports is distinct from any access port's VLAN (so that legitimate native-VLAN traffic is rare or nonexistent), or to tag the native VLAN explicitly so the trunk does not strip it at all, or to avoid trunks to access ports entirely by disabling DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol) and forcing access-mode operation on all non-trunk ports. Option B captures these configuration changes. Option A (SNMP) is unrelated. Option C (more VLANs) does not address the hopping mechanism. Option D (STP) is for loop prevention, not VLAN security. The CISSP exam tests VLAN hopping because it is a specific, testable example of L2 security weaknesses and because VLAN-based segmentation is weaker than many assume. Private VLANs (PVLAN) add another layer of isolation within a VLAN, restricting even L2 communication between ports in the same VLAN.

questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing a proposal to use DNS over HTTPS (DoH) for internal DNS queries. The security team is concerned about loss of visibility into DNS traffic. Which MOST accurately describes the trade-off?

options.A: DoH is always better than traditional DNS
options.B: DoH provides confidentiality for DNS queries and prevents network-level tampering, but it bypasses DNS-based security monitoring (DNS logging, DNS firewalls, DNS sinkholing) that many organizations rely on. The resolution is to operate an internal DoH resolver that provides the confidentiality benefit while preserving visibility, or to block external DoH and force DNS through the corporate resolver

questions.rationale: DoH encrypts DNS queries between client and resolver and typically uses port 443 (indistinguishable from HTTPS), which provides confidentiality and prevents network-level tampering or observation of DNS traffic. These are privacy benefits. But DoH bypasses corporate DNS security monitoring: if clients use public DoH resolvers (Cloudflare 1.1.1.1, Google 8.8.8.8, Quad9 9.9.9.9), the organization's DNS logs, DNS firewalls (e.g., Infoblox, Cisco Umbrella), and DNS-based sinkholes cannot observe or intervene in the queries. This is a significant loss of detection capability for threats that use DNS (exfiltration, C2, malware command-and-control, phishing detection). The mature response is to operate an internal DoH resolver so that clients use an encrypted channel but resolution still happens through corporate infrastructure, or to block external DoH at the perimeter and force DNS through the corporate resolver. Option B captures the nuanced trade-off. Option A overstates DoH's benefits. Option C is wrong — DoH works. Option D oversimplifies. The CISSP exam tests this trade-off because it is a current operational issue and because it illustrates the tension between user privacy and corporate visibility.

questions.stem: An organization's cloud VPC has a security group that allows SSH (port 22) from 0.0.0.0/0. The operations team argues that SSH is protected by key-based authentication so IP restriction is unnecessary. Which response is MOST ACCURATE?

options.B: Disagree — even though SSH keys resist password-based brute force, exposing SSH to the entire internet enables reconnaissance, increases the blast radius of credential compromise, and creates log noise that hides real attacks. The correct control is to restrict SSH to specific source IPs (bastion, VPN, corporate CIDR) and use key-based auth plus MFA at the bastion as defense in depth
options.C: Agree if the keys are ECDSA
options.D: Disagree only because SSH should not be used at all

questions.rationale: Exposing SSH to the internet, even with key-based authentication, violates defense in depth. Key-based SSH is resistant to password brute force, but several additional risks remain when the port is exposed: (1) attackers can enumerate SSH versions and identify vulnerable implementations; (2) zero-day vulnerabilities in SSH implementations (OpenSSH has had rare but serious vulnerabilities, e.g., CVE-2024-6387 regreSSHion) put internet-exposed hosts at risk; (3) credential compromise (stolen keys, insider misuse, compromised client) has the full internet as its attack surface rather than a restricted set of source IPs; (4) log volume from continuous scanning obscures real events. The correct control is to restrict SSH source to specific IPs (bastion hosts, VPN endpoints, corporate CIDRs) and enforce additional controls (MFA at the bastion, session recording, time-limited access, key rotation). Option B captures this. Option A trades defense in depth for a single layer. Option C (ECDSA) is about key type, not exposure. Option D (don't use SSH) is too absolute. The CISSP exam tests cloud security group misconfiguration because it is one of the most common cloud incident root causes.

questions.stem: A CISSP reviews a proposal to deploy a new VPN for contractors. The proposal uses pre-shared keys for both authentication and encryption keys, with a single shared password for all contractors. Which is the MOST SIGNIFICANT concern?

options.A: Pre-shared keys are slower than certificates
options.B: A single shared password for all contractors means compromise of one contractor's key compromises all contractor access, there is no revocation granularity (dismissing one contractor requires rotating the key for all), and there is no accountability (no way to tell which contractor used the VPN); the design should use per-contractor certificates with individual enrollment, revocation, and logging
options.C: Pre-shared keys are deprecated in IPsec
options.D: The VPN should use split tunneling

questions.rationale: A shared pre-shared key across all contractors combines three failures: (1) no per-user isolation — compromise of one contractor's credential (phishing, insider misuse, laptop theft, negligent sharing) compromises access for all contractors, effectively turning the shared key into a shared secret with a large and untrustworthy audience; (2) no granular revocation — dismissing a single contractor requires rotating the shared key and redistributing it to everyone who remains, which is operationally painful and often delayed; (3) no accountability — VPN logs cannot attribute activity to individual contractors, defeating audit and investigation. The correct design issues a per-contractor credential (certificate from a trusted CA, per-user key pair, or user-specific EAP method), enrolls contractors individually, logs per-user activity, and revokes on termination. Option B captures these concerns. Option A (speed) is not the issue. Option C (deprecation) is not accurate — PSKs are still supported in IPsec but should be avoided for multi-user scenarios. Option D (split tunneling) is a separate design choice. The CISSP exam tests per-user credentialing because it is a foundational access control principle.

questions.stem: A CISSP reviews a network design that uses the same firewall ruleset for inbound internet traffic and east-west traffic between tiers. Which is the PRIMARY concern?

options.B: Unified rulesets force compromises between internet-facing protections (which tend to be restrictive) and internal traffic patterns (which require richer allowed flows); the correct pattern is separate policies per zone, with each ruleset tailored to its actual traffic and default-deny where applicable
options.D: Firewalls cannot protect against all attacks

questions.rationale: Applying a single ruleset to both internet-facing and east-west traffic forces trade-offs that compromise both: the internet-facing policy needs to be highly restrictive (default-deny, narrowly opened ports, explicit allow lists for specific applications), while east-west policies need richer flows between trusted tiers. A unified ruleset either becomes too permissive (weakening internet protection) or too restrictive (breaking internal functions). The correct approach is separate policies per zone — an internet-facing policy on perimeter firewalls, east-west policies on internal firewalls or micro-segmentation, service-mesh policies for application-level flows — each tailored to the specific traffic and risk profile of that zone. Default-deny should apply to each layer, with explicit allows for known good flows. Option B captures this. Options A and C are generic and do not address the unified-ruleset issue specifically. Option D is a generic disclaimer. The CISSP exam tests zone-based policy because it is core to modern network security and because candidates often default to 'one big firewall' when the right answer is 'many tailored policies'.

questions.stem: A ransomware incident has compromised a single user workstation. Within hours, the ransomware has propagated to 200 file servers, 50 database servers, and multiple domain controllers. What is the MOST LIKELY root cause in the network architecture?

options.A: The antivirus was out of date
options.B: The internal network was flat, with minimal segmentation and overly-permissive east-west traffic, allowing the ransomware to reach high-value systems via SMB, RDP, and WinRM once the initial host was compromised; mature defenses require micro-segmentation, least-privilege SMB/RDP/WinRM access, and isolation of domain controllers

questions.rationale: Ransomware's destructive power comes from lateral movement, and the defining architectural failure in most catastrophic ransomware incidents is a flat network with over-permissive east-west traffic. Once the initial host is compromised, the malware uses legitimate Windows protocols (SMB for file shares, RDP for remote desktop, WinRM for PowerShell remoting) to spread to other systems. In a flat network, every file server, database server, and domain controller is reachable from every user workstation, giving the ransomware a rich target set. The correct architectural defenses are: micro-segmentation that limits east-west traffic to only required flows; specific restriction of SMB, RDP, and WinRM between user workstations and servers (only jump-server access for administrators, with MFA and logging); isolation of domain controllers from user network segments entirely; and tiered administration (Tier 0 for DCs, Tier 1 for servers, Tier 2 for user workstations) with cross-tier access mediated by controls. Option B captures this root cause. Options A, C, and D are contributing factors but not the root architectural cause. The CISSP exam tests this because it is the defining ransomware lesson of 2019-2024 and because the architectural answer is more durable than the individual-control answer.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating a SASE (Secure Access Service Edge) platform that bundles SD-WAN, ZTNA, CASB, SWG, and DLP in a cloud-delivered service. Which is the MOST IMPORTANT consideration in the evaluation?

options.B: Whether the platform's service edges are geographically close to users (for latency), whether it supports the organization's identity provider and policy granularity, whether it provides the inspection depth required (TLS decryption, content inspection), and how operational control and observability work in a managed multi-tenant service

questions.rationale: SASE/SSE evaluation involves multiple real considerations that map to real risk and operational decisions. Latency depends on service edge topology: if users are globally distributed, the platform must have points of presence close to them. Identity integration determines whether policy can be enforced on the organization's actual principals (Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace, on-prem AD via sync). Policy granularity determines whether the platform can express the rules the organization actually needs. Inspection depth determines what the platform can actually see: many threats are in TLS-encrypted traffic, which requires TLS decryption (with cert pinning workarounds, privacy considerations, and performance implications). Operational control and observability are critical because a managed multi-tenant service is a black box by default, and the organization must be able to see its own logs, control its own policies, and troubleshoot its own issues. Option B captures these serious criteria. Options A, C, and D are jokes highlighting the irrelevant factors that should not drive evaluation. The CISSP exam tests SASE because it is a major current architecture trend and because vendor marketing has run far ahead of operational reality.

questions.stem: A CISSP is asked about the security implications of decrypting TLS traffic at a corporate proxy for inspection. Which trade-off is MOST SIGNIFICANT?

options.B: TLS decryption enables content inspection (DLP, malware detection, threat hunting) but introduces privacy concerns for employees, breaks certificate pinning for some applications, requires the organization to install a trusted root CA on every endpoint, creates a concentrated repository of decrypted traffic that becomes a high-value target, and may conflict with legal requirements around user privacy in some jurisdictions
options.C: TLS decryption is the only defense against malware

questions.rationale: TLS decryption (also called SSL inspection or man-in-the-middle decryption) is a common enterprise security capability that enables content inspection of otherwise-encrypted traffic. It works by terminating TLS at the inspection device and re-establishing it with a certificate issued by a corporate CA that every endpoint trusts. The inspection device sees plaintext and can run DLP, anti-malware, threat-hunting, and content filtering. The trade-offs are significant: (1) privacy — employees' personal communications (banking, health, personal email) can be read by whoever controls the inspection device; (2) certificate pinning — applications that pin specific certificates (banking apps, some SaaS clients, certain mobile apps) will refuse to connect through an inspection proxy, breaking functionality; (3) trust dependency — the corporate root CA becomes a high-value target because its compromise would allow TLS impersonation of any site; (4) decrypted traffic aggregation — the inspection device sees all decrypted traffic, creating a concentrated repository that is itself a breach risk; (5) legal — some jurisdictions restrict employer interception of employee communications without explicit notice and consent. Option B captures the compound trade-off. Option A understates utility; Options C and D overstate it. The CISSP exam tests TLS decryption because it is a real operational decision with real ethical and technical consequences.

questions.stem: A CISO is asked whether the organization should adopt post-quantum cryptography (PQC) for its VPN infrastructure immediately after NIST finalized FIPS 203, 204, and 205 in August 2024. Which response is MOST REALISTIC?

options.A: Adopt immediately with full replacement of classical primitives
options.B: Plan for hybrid key exchange (classical ECDH combined with ML-KEM) as the initial transition step, because hybrid provides defense in depth against both classical and quantum attacks; full replacement is a multi-year program that depends on library, hardware, and protocol support across the stack, and the most urgent priority is inventorying where classical public-key crypto is used and designing for crypto-agility
options.C: Ignore PQC because quantum computers don't exist yet
options.D: Replace all cryptography including symmetric primitives

questions.rationale: NIST's post-quantum standards are finalized and available, but full replacement of classical public-key cryptography in a production VPN infrastructure is a multi-year program that depends on library support, hardware acceleration, protocol support in IKE/TLS/SSH, interoperability across diverse endpoints, and organizational readiness. The practical first step is hybrid key exchange: combining classical ECDH with a post-quantum KEM (typically ML-KEM) so that the resulting shared secret is secure if either component is secure. This provides defense in depth during the transition period — if the classical component is broken by a quantum computer, the PQ component still protects the session; if the PQ component has a cryptanalytic weakness discovered later, the classical component still protects. Major VPN and TLS libraries have been implementing hybrid modes (OpenSSL 3.x, BoringSSL, Wolfssl, and others). In parallel, the organization should inventory where classical public-key crypto is used, prioritize by data longevity and exposure, and build crypto-agility into every new system. Option B captures the realistic phased approach. Option A is operationally premature. Option C ignores the 'harvest now, decrypt later' threat. Option D confuses the problem — symmetric crypto (AES-256) is largely already considered quantum-resistant with appropriate key sizes, so replacing it is not a priority.

questions.stem: A CISSP is asked to explain why deep packet inspection (DPI) at a corporate firewall is increasingly ineffective against modern threats. Which is the MOST ACCURATE explanation?

options.B: The rise of end-to-end TLS encryption (HTTPS by default, TLS 1.3, QUIC, certificate pinning, HTTP/3 over QUIC/UDP) means that a growing fraction of traffic is opaque to perimeter DPI. Defenders must shift to endpoint visibility (EDR), identity-aware proxies, behavioral analysis, and TLS decryption where legally and operationally feasible
options.C: DPI is broken because it is slow
options.D: DPI should be replaced with firewalls

questions.rationale: Deep packet inspection historically allowed firewalls to see application-layer content — recognizing HTTP requests, analyzing DNS queries, detecting protocol-specific attack signatures — but the dominant trend of the last decade has been encryption by default. TLS is now ubiquitous; HTTPS is the default for web; TLS 1.3 is standard and includes encryption of extensions that previously leaked information (SNI is being encrypted via Encrypted Client Hello, ECH); QUIC runs over UDP and encrypts transport metadata that TCP left visible; HTTP/3 runs over QUIC. The practical result is that a growing fraction of corporate network traffic is opaque to perimeter DPI without breaking encryption (which has its own trade-offs). The defensive response is layered: (1) endpoint visibility via EDR agents that see decrypted content at the process level, (2) identity-aware proxies that inspect application-layer traffic they legitimately terminate, (3) behavioral analysis on metadata (volume, timing, connection patterns, DNS queries), (4) selective TLS decryption with the trade-offs already discussed, and (5) upstream controls at SaaS and cloud provider edges where content is unencrypted in provider infrastructure. Option B captures the strategic shift. Option A is wrong. Option C (speed) is a minor consideration. Option D is nonsensical.

questions.stem: A CISSP is designing network security for a new Kubernetes cluster that runs customer-facing services. Which of the following is MOST IMPORTANT to implement?

options.A: Place all pods in the default namespace
options.B: Enforce network policies (Kubernetes NetworkPolicy or service mesh sidecars) that default-deny inter-pod traffic and explicitly allow only the flows required by the application; combine with a service mesh for identity-aware service-to-service authorization, and with a CNI plugin that enforces network policies (Calico, Cilium, AWS VPC CNI)
options.D: Disable all security features for performance

questions.rationale: Kubernetes clusters are multi-tenant by design (at the namespace level at minimum, often cluster-wide), and by default pods can talk to any other pod in the cluster — a flat network inside the cluster. Without explicit network policies, a compromised pod can reach every other pod, making lateral movement easy. The correct pattern is to enforce NetworkPolicy resources that default-deny all inter-pod traffic and explicitly allow only the flows required. For richer policies including identity-aware service-to-service authorization, a service mesh (Istio, Linkerd, or similar) adds sidecar proxies that enforce policies based on service identity (via mTLS with SPIFFE identities), not just network identity. The CNI plugin must support NetworkPolicy enforcement: Calico, Cilium, and AWS VPC CNI (with recent versions) do; the default Flannel does not. Combining these gives micro-segmentation at the Kubernetes level. Option B captures this. Options A, C, and D are security anti-patterns. The CISSP exam increasingly tests Kubernetes security because it is ubiquitous in modern infrastructure and because its network defaults differ from traditional server networks. Related concepts: Pod Security Admission (replacing PodSecurityPolicy), Open Policy Agent for admission control, and mTLS between services via service mesh.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating whether an organization's BGP configuration is resilient to hijacks. Which combination of controls is MOST EFFECTIVE?

options.A: No controls are needed because BGP is self-securing
options.B: Publish signed Route Origin Authorizations (ROAs) for owned prefixes via RPKI, configure upstream providers to honor RPKI-ROV so invalid announcements are dropped, implement prefix filtering at peering points, monitor BGP announcements via RIPE RIS/BGPmon/RouteViews and configure alerts for unauthorized announcements of owned prefixes

questions.rationale: BGP resilience requires both cryptographic validation and operational monitoring. Publishing ROAs via RPKI provides a signed statement that specific Autonomous Systems are authorized to originate specific prefixes; with Route Origin Validation (ROV) configured at ISPs and backbone operators, invalid announcements can be dropped, dramatically reducing the impact of origin hijacks. Prefix filtering at peering points ensures that only expected prefixes are accepted from specific peers, reducing leak risk. Monitoring via public BGP observation services (RIPE RIS, BGPmon, RouteViews, Team Cymru) allows detection of unauthorized announcements of the organization's prefixes from anywhere in the world, with alerts triggering investigation and remediation. The combination provides cryptographic validation plus operational detection. Option B captures this layered approach. Option A is wrong — BGP is not self-securing and unintentional and intentional hijacks are common. Option C (IPv6) does not address BGP's trust model. Option D is not a structural solution. The CISSP exam tests BGP because it remains one of the most under-secured critical internet protocols, and CISSPs advising large organizations need to understand both the technical and operational dimensions of BGP hygiene. RPKI adoption has grown significantly since 2020, with major transit providers implementing ROV, but full deployment remains incomplete.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating the security of a new IoT deployment. The devices communicate over LTE-M using mutual TLS. The security team is concerned about device-to-device lateral movement if one device is compromised. Which mitigation is MOST IMPORTANT?

options.B: Ensure each device has a unique identity tied to a hardware root of trust, enforce per-device certificates with individual revocation, and constrain each device's cloud-side authorization to only the specific APIs and topics it legitimately requires — so that a compromised device cannot impersonate others or access resources beyond its own scope

questions.rationale: IoT device-to-device lateral movement is primarily controlled at the cloud side, not on the devices themselves. The key pattern is: (1) per-device unique identity tied to a hardware root of trust (secure element, TPM-equivalent, or similar) so that the identity cannot be extracted or cloned; (2) per-device certificates with individual enrollment, issuance, and revocation workflows; (3) cloud-side authorization policies that constrain each device's permissions to only the specific APIs, message topics (in MQTT), or resources it legitimately requires. With these controls, a compromised device can only affect its own authorized resources; it cannot impersonate other devices, access other devices' data, or perform operations beyond its scope. Option B captures this. Option A (device storage encryption) addresses a different threat. Option C (password rotation) does not apply to certificate-based mutual TLS. Option D (antivirus on each device) is impractical for most IoT hardware and does not address the lateral movement concern. The CISSP exam tests IoT because it is a large and growing attack surface and because architectural patterns differ from traditional server networks. Related: AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, and similar cloud IoT services provide per-device policy enforcement as a native feature.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating a design for a new multi-region deployment. The design uses public DNS load balancing (GeoDNS) to route users to the nearest region. Which consideration is MOST IMPORTANT from a security standpoint?

options.B: Public DNS responses can be observed, spoofed (if DNSSEC is not used), and affect routing of users to potentially malicious endpoints; the design should include DNSSEC signing for the authoritative records, health checks that remove compromised regions from rotation automatically, and cross-region failover that does not rely on a single DNS provider as a single point of failure
options.D: Only IPv6 DNS should be used

questions.rationale: DNS-based load balancing and routing depends on DNS responses that can be observed, cached, and potentially manipulated. The security-relevant considerations for the design are: (1) DNSSEC signing of authoritative records so that resolvers can verify integrity; (2) health checks that automatically remove compromised or unreachable regions from the DNS rotation so users are not routed to bad endpoints; (3) multi-provider DNS to avoid a single-provider failure (Dyn 2016 outage affected many major services because they depended on a single DNS provider); (4) short TTLs on DNS records to enable fast failover when a region is removed from rotation, balanced against the load on authoritative servers; (5) privacy considerations for the routing metadata exposed to DNS. Option B captures the compound architectural consideration. Option A dismisses DNS security. Options C and D are not operationally viable. The CISSP exam tests DNS architecture because it is a critical, widely-used, and widely-misconfigured component of network design.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating whether to use IPv6 in a new network. A team member argues that IPv6 is inherently more secure than IPv4 because 'IPsec is built in'. Which response is MOST ACCURATE?

options.A: IPv6 is unconditionally more secure than IPv4
options.B: IPv6 was originally specified with IPsec as a mandatory feature, but subsequent updates (RFC 6434) made it optional in practice. IPv6 has its own security challenges: larger attack surface from new protocols (ICMPv6 is required and cannot be globally blocked), SLAAC abuse, rogue RA attacks, transition mechanisms (6to4, Teredo) that can tunnel unexpected traffic, and operational immaturity in many environments. The correct framing is that IPv6 neither provides nor undermines security by itself; security depends on design and operation

questions.rationale: The 'IPv6 has IPsec built in' claim is a common misconception. The original IPv6 specification (RFC 2460) required IPsec support in conformant implementations, which created the impression that IPv6 was inherently secure. Subsequent updates (RFC 6434) downgraded this to 'should' — IPsec is available but not universally enabled, and in practice most IPv6 deployments do not rely on IPsec for payload protection any more than IPv4 does. IPv6 has several security considerations that differ from IPv4: (1) ICMPv6 is required for basic function (neighbor discovery, path MTU, etc.) and cannot be globally blocked without breaking the network; (2) SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration) can be abused by rogue RA attacks; (3) transition mechanisms like 6to4 and Teredo can tunnel IPv6 traffic inside IPv4 in unexpected ways; (4) many organizations have less operational maturity in IPv6 than IPv4. Option B captures the accurate picture. Options A, C, and D are oversimplified. The CISSP exam tests IPv6 because it is increasingly deployed and because the 'built-in security' myth persists. The mature framing: IPv6 is neither more nor less secure than IPv4 by default; it requires its own security design.

questions.stem: A CISSP is asked whether the organization should implement CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker) and, if so, whether to deploy it in API mode, forward proxy mode, or reverse proxy mode. Which is the BEST response?

options.A: CASB is not needed if you have a firewall
options.B: CASB is valuable for SaaS visibility and control. API mode inspects data already in SaaS via vendor APIs (good for sanctioned apps, bad for real-time control of unsanctioned). Forward proxy mode inspects user traffic en route to SaaS (good for in-line control, requires traffic steering). Reverse proxy mode inspects traffic to known SaaS (requires SaaS-specific support). A mature deployment typically combines API mode for sanctioned apps with forward proxy for broader visibility into unsanctioned SaaS, integrated with ZTNA and SWG in a SASE architecture

questions.rationale: Cloud Access Security Brokers provide visibility and control over SaaS applications — the major blind spot in traditional perimeter security. The three deployment modes each address different aspects of the problem. API mode uses vendor APIs (Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Salesforce, Slack, etc.) to inspect data already in the SaaS application: content DLP, shared-file scanning, anomaly detection. It is post-hoc (not in-line) but has full visibility into sanctioned apps. Forward proxy mode sits between users and SaaS, inspecting traffic in flight — it provides real-time control including DLP on outbound data and access control to unsanctioned apps. Reverse proxy mode requires SaaS-vendor support (SAML-integrated proxy) and works only for specific applications. Mature deployments combine API mode for sanctioned apps (deep content visibility) with forward proxy for broader visibility into unsanctioned SaaS and in-line control, integrated with ZTNA and SWG in a SASE architecture. Option B captures this nuanced framing. Option A dismisses a legitimate tool class. Option C is oversimplified. Option D is wrong. The CISSP exam tests CASB because SaaS governance is a growing area and because CASB mode selection has real operational and visibility consequences.

questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing a VoIP deployment and notes that SIP signaling is in cleartext but RTP media is SRTP-encrypted. Which concern is MOST SIGNIFICANT?

options.B: Cleartext SIP exposes call metadata (who called whom, when, how long), permits call redirection and call hijacking by attackers on the signaling path, and enables toll fraud by exploiting the signaling protocol; the design should add SIPS (SIP over TLS) to encrypt signaling, with mutual authentication of endpoints

questions.rationale: SRTP encrypts the media stream (voice content) but leaves the signaling (SIP) in the clear. Cleartext SIP exposes several things: (1) call metadata, including who called whom, when, and for how long — significant for privacy and for pattern-of-life analysis; (2) call setup details that an attacker on the path can manipulate to redirect calls, inject content, or terminate sessions; (3) authentication credentials if SIP uses digest auth over the plain channel; (4) signaling-level vulnerabilities that enable toll fraud by exploiting features like call forwarding or conferencing. The correct defense is SIPS (SIP over TLS) which encrypts the signaling channel just as SRTP encrypts the media. Both should be used together, with mutual authentication of endpoints to prevent signaling injection. Option B captures this. Option A mis-states the design intent. Option C is wrong — SRTP works. Option D is too absolute. The CISSP exam tests VoIP security because voice is a common overlooked area and because signaling vs media is a clear distinction that mirrors control-plane vs data-plane distinctions elsewhere. Related: ZRTP for keyagreement without PKI dependencies, and the general principle that metadata can be as sensitive as content in communications systems.

questions.stem: A security architect must choose between allowing internet-facing services to be accessed directly or routing them through a reverse proxy. Which advantage is the MOST IMPORTANT in a mature design?

options.B: A reverse proxy provides a policy enforcement point where TLS is terminated, requests are inspected (WAF), rate limited, authenticated, and logged; the origin server is hidden from direct exposure, reducing attack surface; and operational concerns (load balancing, caching, compression) are centralized. This is a classic defense-in-depth and least-common-mechanism pattern
options.D: Reverse proxies are required by law

questions.rationale: A reverse proxy in front of internet-facing services provides multiple layered benefits. At the security layer: TLS is terminated at the proxy, request content can be inspected by a WAF for injection/XSS/SSRF/OWASP Top 10 attacks, rate limiting prevents abuse, authentication can be enforced before requests reach the origin, and detailed logging provides visibility into every request. At the architectural layer: the origin server is hidden from direct internet exposure, which reduces attack surface (attackers must compromise the proxy before reaching the origin) and provides isolation (zero-day vulnerabilities in the origin are not directly exploitable). At the operational layer: load balancing, caching, compression, and connection pooling are centralized. Option B captures the compound benefit. Option A (speed) is secondary. Option C (cost) may be true but is not the primary security rationale. Option D (legally required) is not accurate as a general statement. The CISSP exam tests reverse proxies because they are a foundational pattern for internet-facing services and because they illustrate defense in depth, least common mechanism, and centralized policy enforcement.

questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing a managed service provider's remote management access to customer networks. The MSP uses a shared jumpbox with a single admin account. Which is the MOST SIGNIFICANT concern?

options.B: Shared admin accounts at an MSP create severe accountability, isolation, and compromise-radius risks: one engineer's compromised credentials reveal access to all customer networks, audit logs cannot attribute actions to specific engineers, and rotation is burdensome. The design should require per-engineer accounts, MFA, session recording on the jumpbox, time-limited access tickets, and customer-side segmentation of MSP access
options.D: Customers should do their own management

questions.rationale: A shared admin account at a managed service provider is one of the most consequential misconfigurations possible in a B2B security arrangement. The failures compound: (1) no accountability — when something goes wrong, the MSP and customer cannot determine which engineer was responsible; (2) no isolation — one engineer's credential compromise exposes access to every customer network the MSP manages, creating a supply-chain attack surface for attackers who target MSPs specifically to reach their customers; (3) rotation burden — changing a shared password requires coordinating with everyone who holds it, which typically means rotations are infrequent; (4) insider risk — a single malicious or careless engineer has unrestricted access to all customer environments. The correct design requires per-engineer accounts (tied to the engineer's identity, not a role), MFA with hardware tokens, session recording on the jumpbox for accountability and forensic review, time-limited access tickets tied to specific change requests, and customer-side segmentation and logging of MSP access (so customers can monitor the MSP's own activity in their environments). Option B captures these concerns. Options A, C, and D are inadequate. The CISSP exam tests MSP access because MSP compromises have been high-profile (Kaseya 2021, SolarWinds 2020).

questions.stem: A CISSP is asked about the role of network-level threat intelligence (IP reputation feeds, domain blocklists, malware indicators) in defense. Which framing is MOST ACCURATE?

options.A: Threat intel is sufficient for defense
options.B: Threat intel feeds are valuable for catching known bad actors and indicators of known malware/phishing campaigns, but they catch only what is already known; determined adversaries will use unknown infrastructure, new domains, and previously-unseen indicators. Threat intel should be one layer of a defense-in-depth architecture that also includes behavioral analysis, zero-trust access controls, segmentation, and continuous monitoring
options.D: Threat intel only works if you buy expensive feeds

questions.rationale: Network threat intelligence — lists of malicious IPs, domains, file hashes, URLs, and other indicators of compromise — is a useful but limited defense. The strength is that it catches known threats cheaply and at scale: commodity malware, known phishing infrastructure, known C2 domains, and IOCs from published incident reports. The weakness is that determined adversaries rotate infrastructure rapidly, use compromised but legitimate sites, and can outpace threat intel feeds. Threat intel should be one layer of a defense-in-depth strategy that also includes: (1) behavioral analysis that catches anomalies regardless of whether specific indicators are known, (2) zero-trust access controls that limit what a compromised host can do, (3) segmentation that limits lateral movement, (4) continuous monitoring and threat hunting that proactively searches for unknown threats, and (5) endpoint detection that operates at the process level rather than the network level. Option B captures this layered framing. Options A, C, and D are oversimplified. The CISSP exam tests threat intelligence because it is a popular tool category and because practitioners over-rely on it as a complete defense.

questions.stem: A CISSP discovers that an organization's internal network still uses NetBIOS, LLMNR, and mDNS for name resolution, alongside DNS. Which attack is MOST DIRECTLY enabled by these legacy protocols?

options.B: LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning: an attacker responds to broadcast name resolution requests with their own address, tricking Windows clients into authenticating to the attacker; combined with relay tools like Responder and ntlmrelayx, this enables credential capture and relay to other services. The mitigation is to disable LLMNR, NetBIOS, and mDNS organization-wide and rely exclusively on DNS

questions.rationale: Legacy Microsoft name resolution protocols — NetBIOS Name Service (NBT-NS), Link-Local Multicast Name Resolution (LLMNR), and mDNS — fall back when standard DNS cannot resolve a name. They rely on broadcast or multicast, meaning any host on the same L2 segment can respond. An attacker running Responder or similar tools answers every broadcast query with their own address, and when a Windows client attempts to authenticate to the 'resolved' host using NTLM, the attacker captures the NTLM challenge-response. The captured hash can be cracked offline or relayed in real time (ntlmrelayx) to other services that accept NTLM authentication, enabling lateral movement and privilege escalation. These attacks are widely used in red-team engagements and real-world breaches. The mitigation is to disable LLMNR and NetBIOS via Group Policy organization-wide, disable mDNS where not needed, use DNS exclusively, enable SMB signing, and enable LDAP channel binding and signing to prevent relay. Option B captures this correctly. Options A, C, and D are unrelated. The CISSP exam tests LLMNR/NBT-NS because it is one of the most common Active Directory attack paths and because the defense (disable legacy protocols) is simple and well-known but often not implemented.

questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing a design that places a corporate firewall in front of a Kubernetes ingress controller. The team wants all application-layer inspection to happen at the firewall. Which is the MOST SIGNIFICANT concern with this design?

options.B: Modern Kubernetes architectures encourage application-layer policy enforcement at the ingress controller and service mesh layers, where identity (mTLS with service identities), content (HTTP semantics), and policy (application-aware rules) can be combined. Centralizing all application-layer inspection at a perimeter firewall creates a chokepoint that is duplicative of ingress-layer controls, does not see mesh-internal traffic, and may not understand modern application protocols; a balanced design places network-level controls at the firewall and application-level policies at the ingress and mesh

questions.rationale: Kubernetes deployments operate at multiple layers, each with its own natural policy enforcement point. The perimeter firewall (outside the cluster) is appropriate for coarse-grained network-level controls: IP allowlists, geographic restrictions, rate limiting, and basic DDoS protection. The ingress controller (inside the cluster) is appropriate for application-layer policies: HTTP routing, TLS termination, WAF rules, authentication enforcement, and request-level rate limiting with identity awareness. The service mesh (between pods inside the cluster) provides mTLS, per-service identity, and fine-grained service-to-service authorization. Each layer sees traffic the other layers do not. Centralizing all application-layer inspection at the perimeter firewall means: (1) the firewall duplicates work that the ingress controller already does natively; (2) the firewall does not see mesh-internal traffic at all; (3) the firewall may not understand modern protocols (HTTP/3, gRPC over HTTP/2, WebSocket), creating blind spots. The correct design is layered: network controls at the firewall, application controls at the ingress, service-to-service controls at the mesh. Option B captures this layered framing. Options A, C, and D are oversimplified.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating a vendor claim that their firewall provides 'inline deep-learning threat detection with no false positives'. Which is the MOST APPROPRIATE response?

options.A: Accept the claim at face value
options.B: 'No false positives' is not a credible claim for any inline machine-learning detection system. All detection systems trade false positives against false negatives, and 'no false positives' typically means 'we tune to high precision and miss things' or 'our marketing is unreliable'. The correct evaluation asks about precision/recall trade-offs, how the system is tuned in customer environments, how false positives are handled operationally, and what the training and update cycle looks like

questions.rationale: Any inline detection system — signature-based, heuristic, machine learning, or hybrid — involves a trade-off between false positives (benign traffic classified as malicious) and false negatives (malicious traffic classified as benign). A system tuned to zero false positives typically has high false negatives (misses real attacks) because any ambiguous case is classified as benign to preserve the zero-false-positive property. Conversely, a system tuned for zero false negatives is drowning in false positives. The 'no false positives' claim is therefore either marketing fiction, a specific tuning decision, or a claim that deserves skeptical investigation. The correct evaluation asks: what is the precision-recall trade-off; how is the system tuned in customer environments; what is the false-positive rate in comparable deployments; how are false positives handled operationally (manual review, automatic learning feedback, rollback); what is the training data and update cycle; and what happens when the ML model is wrong (failure modes matter). Option B captures the mature response. Options A and D are uncritical. Option C overcorrects. The CISSP exam tests vendor marketing critical evaluation because the CISSP role includes pushing back on unsubstantiated claims.

questions.stem: A CISSP is designing a DMZ for internet-facing services. The team proposes a single-firewall DMZ (one firewall with three interfaces: external, DMZ, internal). A reviewer suggests a dual-firewall DMZ instead. What is the MOST SIGNIFICANT advantage of the dual-firewall approach?

options.B: A dual-firewall DMZ places two separate firewalls (ideally from different vendors) between the internet and the internal network, with the DMZ sitting between them. A compromise or vulnerability in one firewall does not bridge the entire DMZ — the second firewall is still enforcing policy. The design provides defense in depth and isolation of the DMZ from both sides. The trade-off is cost and operational complexity

questions.rationale: A dual-firewall DMZ (also called a screened subnet or 'two firewall' DMZ) is a defense-in-depth architectural pattern. Traffic from the internet passes through the first firewall to reach services in the DMZ; traffic from the DMZ to the internal network passes through a second firewall. The two firewalls enforce separate policies and, in the strongest versions of the pattern, come from different vendors so that a vulnerability in one product is not shared by both. A single-firewall DMZ, by contrast, has one policy enforcement point: a vulnerability or misconfiguration in that single device potentially bridges internet traffic directly to the internal network. The dual approach provides: (1) defense in depth — two independent controls must fail for traffic to traverse; (2) vendor diversity — vulnerabilities in one vendor do not automatically bridge; (3) separation of policies — external and internal policies can be managed separately by different teams if desired. The trade-offs are cost (two firewalls instead of one), operational complexity (two rule sets, two update cycles), and potential performance impact. Option B captures these trade-offs. Options A, C, and D are the opposite of true.
