questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating a proposal to use a single VLAN for all users in a corporate office. The proposer argues that 'VLANs don't matter because we have a good firewall at the perimeter'. Which is the MOST SIGNIFICANT weakness of this reasoning?

options.B: Perimeter firewalls enforce policy on north-south traffic (internet to internal) but cannot enforce policy on east-west traffic (internal to internal), so a flat single-VLAN user network allows any compromised or malicious host to freely reach any other host on the network, enabling lateral movement, broadcast-based attacks (LLMNR poisoning, ARP attacks), and pivoting to high-value systems

questions.rationale: The reasoning error in the proposal is the assumption that perimeter security is the only relevant security. Perimeter firewalls protect the boundary between the internet and the internal network and enforce policy on traffic crossing that boundary — north-south traffic. They do not see and cannot enforce policy on traffic that stays inside the internal network — east-west traffic. A flat single-VLAN user network means that any compromised user workstation, any malicious insider device, any rogue BYOD, and any misconfigured guest machine can freely reach every other host on the network: file shares, print servers, administrative consoles, other users' machines, IoT devices. This enables: (1) lateral movement from an initial compromise; (2) broadcast-based attacks like LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning, ARP spoofing, and DHCP attacks that depend on L2 adjacency; (3) pivoting to higher-value systems by attacking insufficiently-hardened adjacent hosts; (4) infiltration of the Active Directory environment from a compromised workstation. The correct design uses VLANs or subnets to segment different categories of hosts (finance, engineering, operations, servers, IoT, guest) and enforces inter-VLAN policy through firewalls or micro-segmentation. Option B captures the reasoning gap. Options A, C, and D are wrong.

questions.stem: A CISSP is auditing a SASE deployment and finds that the organization's endpoint agent forwards traffic to the SASE provider's cloud for inspection. Which is the MOST IMPORTANT privacy and compliance consideration?

options.B: Routing employee traffic through a third-party inspection cloud means the provider has visibility into potentially all encrypted traffic if TLS decryption is enabled, creating concerns around employee privacy laws (especially in jurisdictions with worker consent requirements), data residency (where the inspection cloud operates), data retention (how long the provider keeps decrypted traffic and metadata), and the provider's own security posture; the evaluation must address all these dimensions before deployment

questions.rationale: SASE platforms provide significant security value but introduce privacy and compliance considerations that differ from on-premises inspection. Routing employee traffic through a third-party inspection cloud means: (1) the SASE provider has visibility into traffic metadata at minimum, and into decrypted content if TLS decryption is enabled; (2) employee privacy laws in many jurisdictions (EU under GDPR, Germany with its strong works council tradition, others) may require explicit notice and consent, and/or may restrict specific forms of monitoring; (3) data residency rules may restrict where inspection and retained data can live; (4) data retention of decrypted traffic and metadata at the provider creates a concentrated repository whose breach would have outsized consequences; (5) the provider's own security posture becomes part of the customer's threat model because a compromise of the provider compromises everyone routed through it. The evaluation must address all these dimensions. Option B captures this. Options A, C, and D are oversimplified. The CISSP exam tests these considerations because they are current and significant in real SASE deployments.

questions.stem: A CISSP reviews a proposal to implement network access control (NAC) with 802.1X for all wired and wireless connections. Which of the following is MOST IMPORTANT to get right in the deployment?

options.B: The authentication method (EAP-TLS with certificates is strongest), the authorization policies (what level of access each category of device receives), the posture assessment (health checks for antivirus, patches, encryption), the guest and BYOD enrollment workflows, fallback and recovery procedures for when the NAC service is unavailable, and integration with existing IAM and asset management systems
options.C: The color of the NAC appliance
options.D: Whether the NAC is hardware or software

questions.rationale: NAC deployment is an organizational change with many operational dimensions. Authentication method: EAP-TLS with machine and user certificates is the strongest option because certificates are harder to steal than passwords and the binding to a device identity is strong; PEAP-MSCHAPv2 is an easier-but-weaker alternative that relies on Active Directory passwords. Authorization: the policy determines what level of access each category of device receives (corporate-managed, BYOD, guest, IoT), which must be tuned to the organization's risk tolerance. Posture assessment: checks that devices meet minimum health requirements (antivirus installed and current, OS patches, disk encryption, screen lock) before admitting to the network; must be tuned to avoid excessive remediation friction. Enrollment workflows: guest and BYOD users need a way to onboard without overwhelming helpdesk; self-service enrollment portals help. Fallback and recovery: if the NAC service is unavailable, the network cannot admit any new connections, so a documented fallback (open-fail for emergencies, manual override, backup authentication server) is essential. Integration: NAC must tie to IAM (identity source), MDM (device posture), and asset management (known-device inventory). Option B captures these dimensions. Options A, C, and D are frivolous. The CISSP exam tests NAC because the deployment quality determines whether it adds security or just operational friction.

questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing a design that uses a single wildcard TLS certificate for all subdomains of a large corporate domain. Which is the MOST SIGNIFICANT concern?

options.B: A single wildcard certificate means the corresponding private key is used across many services; compromise of any single service reveals the key and compromises TLS for every subdomain; and the blast radius of key compromise is maximal. The modern alternative is per-service certificates via automated issuance (ACME/Let's Encrypt, AWS ACM, internal CA), which limits the blast radius of any single compromise
options.C: Wildcard certificates do not work with modern browsers

questions.rationale: Wildcard TLS certificates cover '*.example.com' and can be used on any subdomain of example.com. This has operational benefits (one certificate to manage, works for newly-added subdomains without re-issuance) but a significant security weakness: the same private key is used on every service that uses the wildcard. Compromise of any single service (privilege escalation, misconfigured backup, leaked CI/CD artifact, stolen image) reveals the key and compromises TLS for every subdomain that shares it. The blast radius of a single compromise is maximal. The modern alternative is automated per-service certificate issuance via ACME (Let's Encrypt, ZeroSSL, internal ACME servers), AWS Certificate Manager, or internal certificate automation: each service gets its own certificate, rotation is automated, and compromise of one service does not expose others. Option B captures this. Option A is true but secondary. Options C and D are wrong — wildcards work and are not deprecated, just considered weaker than per-service certificates for mature deployments. The CISSP exam tests this because it is a clear trade-off between convenience and security, and because automated certificate management has made per-service certificates the new default for many organizations.

questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing a network design for a new IoT platform. The platform uses MQTT over TLS with mutual authentication. A reviewer asks whether MQTT topic structure itself is a security consideration. Which response is MOST ACCURATE?

options.A: MQTT topics are irrelevant to security
options.B: MQTT topic structure is a first-class security concern because topics define the message routing space and authorization is typically enforced at the topic level; a poorly-designed topic hierarchy can expose device-to-device messages to unauthorized subscribers, create authorization-bypass risks via wildcard subscriptions, and make fine-grained access control difficult. The design should use a hierarchical topic structure with per-device namespaces and enforce topic-level ACLs

questions.rationale: MQTT's topic-based publish-subscribe model means that authorization is typically expressed as 'who can publish to topic X' and 'who can subscribe to topic X'. The topic structure directly determines the security boundaries: a flat topic space makes fine-grained access control difficult, and wildcard subscriptions (like 'devices/#' which subscribes to all device subtopics) can expose messages from all devices to a single subscriber. A well-designed topic hierarchy uses per-device namespaces ('devices/{deviceId}/telemetry', 'devices/{deviceId}/commands'), per-service namespaces, and explicit authorization at each level. Topic-level ACLs in the MQTT broker (EMQX, Mosquitto with ACL plugins, AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub) enforce that each device can publish and subscribe only to its own topics and nothing else. This prevents: (1) a compromised device from reading other devices' messages; (2) a compromised subscriber from receiving messages outside its authorized scope; (3) accidental data leaks through overly-broad subscriptions. Option B captures this. Option A dismisses a real concern. Options C and D are wrong. The CISSP exam tests MQTT because it is a common IoT protocol and because its topic-based authorization model differs from request-response protocols.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating a corporate network that has historically relied on 'defense in depth' as its primary security framing. The CISSP is asked whether the organization should also adopt 'zero trust'. Which framing BEST describes the relationship?

options.A: Zero trust replaces defense in depth
options.B: Defense in depth and zero trust are complementary, not competing. Defense in depth is a principle about layering independent controls so no single failure is catastrophic; zero trust is an architecture about removing implicit network trust and authenticating every access per-request. A mature security program applies both: zero trust principles reshape how identity, device, and policy are evaluated, while defense in depth ensures that any single control failure is survivable. The two together are stronger than either alone

questions.rationale: Defense in depth is a longstanding security principle: layer multiple independent controls so that failure of any single control does not lead to a catastrophic outcome. Zero trust is a specific architectural approach (formalized in NIST SP 800-207) that removes implicit trust based on network location and requires authentication and authorization per-request. The two are complementary, not competing. Zero trust architecture includes multiple layers of control — identity verification, device posture assessment, policy evaluation, per-request authorization, continuous monitoring — which is itself defense in depth. Defense in depth alone, without zero trust's per-request authorization, can still leave implicit network trust intact. A mature security program applies both: zero trust principles reshape how identity, device, and policy are evaluated (eliminating 'trusted network' zones), while defense in depth ensures that any single control failure is survivable (so a compromise of the policy engine does not catastrophically fail the system). Option B captures this integrated view. Option A misstates the relationship. Option C is wrong — defense in depth remains a foundational principle. Option D dismisses a legitimate architectural approach. The CISSP exam tests this because practitioners sometimes treat zero trust as a replacement for prior practice rather than as an evolution that coexists with traditional layered defense.
